title: Room Square Footage Guide
summary: Understand equipment footprint, aisle multiplier, growth reserve, and why usable room area is larger than the hardware footprint.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the planning area needed for equipment, access paths, and future expansion so the room is not sized only around the devices themselves.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before the layout, rack, pathway, or support-room decision gets locked in.
Use it to document the assumptions behind a physical infrastructure decision.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble or rework.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning equipment rooms, network closets, security head-end rooms, small server rooms, or any space where racks, wall fields, UPS gear, or support equipment need serviceable floor area.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, installer, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on the Room Square Footage Planner and explain how each value affects the required room area.

items.label: Equipment Footprint (sq ft)
items.description: The physical floor area occupied by racks, cabinets, wall fields, battery cabinets, support equipment, or other fixed infrastructure before clearance and growth are added.
examples: Existing equipment footprint from a site survey
items.whyItMatters: This is the base area the room must support before aisles, service clearance, and future expansion are considered.
items.commonMistake: Using only the equipment footprint as the final room size.

links.label: Compare with UPS Room Sizing
links.note: Use when UPS or battery cabinets are part of the support-space plan.

items.label: Clearance / Aisle Factor (×)
items.description: A multiplier that accounts for access aisles, service clearance, working space, door swing, and maneuvering room around the equipment.
examples: Higher factor for crowded equipment rooms
items.whyItMatters: The same equipment footprint can require very different room sizes depending on the access standard and service expectations.
items.commonMistake: Treating clearance as leftover space instead of a planned requirement.

links.label: See equipment spacing logic
links.note: Useful when aisle widths and rack rows are being modeled directly.

items.label: Growth Reserve (%)
items.description: Extra room area reserved for future racks, equipment, support gear, or project expansion after the current footprint and clearance are calculated.
items.whyItMatters: Growth reserve keeps a day-one room from becoming full as soon as the next expansion starts.
items.commonMistake: Leaving no room for future cabinets, cabling, batteries, or service equipment.

links.label: Check rack unit reserve
links.note: Use when the growth concern is rack-space capacity rather than floor area.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the room area has practical reserve. Watch means clearance or growth assumptions are getting tight. Risk means the room may be undersized for the equipment and service access expected.
bullets: Healthy: the area plan leaves usable operating margin.
Watch: verify aisles, working clearances, growth, and equipment layout before committing.
Risk: increase room size, reduce equipment footprint, or validate the design with a detailed layout.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Infrastructure planning problems usually come from treating the room, rack, pathway, power support, and service space as separate decisions. In the field, those limits stack together.
bullets: Sizing only the equipment and forgetting clearance, access, maintenance, and growth.
Using nominal dimensions without adding real cable, airflow, door swing, service, and replacement space.
Assuming a rack, floor, conduit, tray, UPS room, or generator can be pushed to the edge without operational side effects.
Failing to document assumptions before the layout becomes hard or expensive to change.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer installation documents, structural review, electrical design, local code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits and installation instructions still matter.
Local code, AHJ direction, and site standards may change the final requirement.
Existing rooms, pathways, and support infrastructure can create limits that simple math does not show.
A design with reserve space and documented assumptions is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, field measurement, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual room, rack, pathway, equipment, and site conditions.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, service access, installation constraints, and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
